message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/319185535_Real-Time_Modulation_Enhancement_of_Temporal_Envelopes_for_Increasing_Speech_Intelligibility
**Title:** Real-Time Modulation Enhancement of Temporal Envelopes for Increasing Speech Intelligibility
**Found via:** DSP-intelligibility-enhancement search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source content first.

input.prompt: Extract the key findings about modulation enhancement of temporal envelopes for speech intelligibility. Specifically: (1) What method/technique is proposed? (2) What modulation frequencies/bands are enhanced? (3) Quantitative intelligibility improvements (e.g., percentage points, SNR gains, in noise conditions). (4) Whether it works in real-time. (5) Any details about which speech features (consonants, transitions, envelope shape) are emphasized. Provide direct verbatim quotes from the abstract/text. Also note publication date, authors, journal, and whether the full text is accessible or paywalled.

content.content:
The server returned HTTP 403 Forbidden.

The response body was not retrieved. If this URL requires authentication, use an authenticated tool (e.g. `gh` for GitHub, or an MCP-provided fetch tool) instead of WebFetch.

content.text: ResearchGate returned 403. Let me try to find the paper's content through alternative routes.

input.prompt: Find the paper "Real-Time Modulation Enhancement of Temporal Envelopes for Increasing Speech Intelligibility". Report its authors, year, journal/venue, and any abstract text or quantitative intelligibility results shown.

content.content:
# Paper Summary

**Title:** Real-Time Modulation Enhancement of Temporal Envelopes for Increasing Speech Intelligibility

**Authors:** M Koutsogiannaki, H Francois, K Choo, E Oh

**Year:** 2017

**Venue:** Proceedings of Interspeech 2017

**Source:** isca-archive.org

## Abstract Summary

The researchers developed a real-time approach for enhancing speech clarity in noisy environments. Their technique operates across two domains: frequency and time. In the frequency domain, a compression function reallocates energy within frames using novel scaling methods to maintain audio quality. In the time domain, they introduced a mathematical equation that shifts energy from louder to quieter speech segments while preserving overall signal energy.

The authors note that their method "increases the intelligibility of speech while maintaining the overall energy and quality of the speech signal," with evaluations demonstrating improvements in both intelligibility and quality metrics.

**Citation Count:** The paper has been cited by 12 other works, with 5 total versions available in Google Scholar.

content.text: Found it on the ISCA archive. Let me fetch the actual paper from the authoritative ISCA source to get verbatim quotes and quantitative results.

input.prompt: Extract the abstract and key findings verbatim. Specifically: (1) the proposed method for modulation enhancement of temporal envelopes; (2) which modulation frequencies/bands or time/frequency domains are processed; (3) quantitative intelligibility improvements (percentages, scores, noise conditions, listening test results); (4) whether it operates in real-time and at low latency; (5) which speech features are emphasized (transient/quieter segments, consonants, transitions). Provide direct verbatim quotes. Also report authors, year, and venue.

content.content:
# Extraction of Key Information

**Authors, Year, Venue:**
Koutsogiannaki, Francois, Choo, and Oh (2017) at Interspeech 2017

**Proposed Method:**
The authors introduce "a novel approach...for performing real-time speech modulation enhancement to increase speech intelligibility in noise." The technique operates through dual mechanisms: a "compression function is used to perform energy reallocation within a frame" in the frequency domain, and "a mathematical equation is introduced to reallocate energy from the louder to the quieter parts of the speech" in the time domain.

**Domains Processed:**
The method operates "independently in the frequency and time domains."

**Quantitative Improvements:**
No specific numerical percentages or scores are provided in this abstract.

**Real-Time Operation:**
Yes—the method is explicitly described as "real-time" and the authors note they gain "full control of the time-energy reallocation in real-time."

**Speech Features Emphasized:**
The approach reallocates "energy from the louder to the quieter parts of the speech," thus emphasizing quieter segments while "preserving" overall energy characteristics.

**Result Claims:**
"Evaluations on intelligibility and quality show that the suggested approach increases the intelligibility of speech while maintaining the overall energy and quality."

content.text: I have the abstract from the authoritative ISCA archive. The abstract doesn't give specific numbers, but provides the core falsifiable claims. Let me try to access the full PDF for quantitative results.

input.prompt: From the full paper, extract verbatim any quantitative intelligibility results: listening test scores, percentage improvements, word recognition rates, SNR conditions (e.g. speech-shaped noise, competing speaker), comparison baselines (e.g. unmodified speech, SSDRC, other algorithms), and any modulation frequency bands processed. Report exact numbers and quote the sentences.

content.content:
I apologize, but the provided content appears to be a corrupted or improperly encoded PDF file. The text consists primarily of binary data, compression artifacts, and font definitions rather than readable research content.

To extract quantitative intelligibility results, listening test scores, SNR conditions, baselines, and modulation frequency information, I would need access to:

1. A properly decoded/viewable version of the PDF
2. The actual text content of the research paper
3. Results sections with numerical data

**Could you please provide:**
- A clean text extraction from the paper, or
- The paper title and authors so I can help you locate the specific quantitative results, or
- A different file format (e.g., plain text, HTML, or a screenshot of readable sections)

Once readable content is available, I can accurately extract exact numerical results with proper citations in the format you've requested.

cd /Users/fred/.claude/projects/-Users-fred-Software-Engineering-HSC-Textbook-podcast-content/6b1689f5-87d0-40e5-9143-cb0867480812/tool-results/ && (which pdftotext >/dev/null 2>&1 && pdftotext -layout webfetch-1782023215359-z5o72a.pdf - 2>/dev/null | head -300) || python3 -c "
import sys
try:
    from pdfminer.high_level import extract_text
    print(extract_text('webfetch-1782023215359-z5o72a.pdf')[:8000])
except Exception as e:
    print('pdfminer failed:', e)
"

content.text:
I have the full text with all quantitative details. This is a primary peer-reviewed Interspeech 2017 conference paper from Samsung Electronics. I have everything needed to extract falsifiable claims.

Key findings relevant to the research question (angle 4 - DSP enhancement, modulation-envelope enhancement, consonant/quieter-segment boosting):
- RMOD enhances low-frequency temporal modulations by reallocating energy from louder to quieter parts of speech (relevant to consonant/transition emphasis)
- ~2ms delay real-time operation
- Compares to SSDRC; intelligibility gains "approach those of SSDRC" but SSDRC has higher intelligibility gains in all cases while degrading quality
- Critical caveat: intelligibility gains for both algorithms are "quite low when tested with real noises" vs synthetic SSN
- Preserves RMS energy and quality unlike SSDRC

These directly bear on angle 4 (modulation-envelope enhancement, energy reallocation to quieter segments) and provide the important nuance that gains shrink in realistic conditions.

claims.claim: Enhancing the low-frequency temporal modulations of speech by reallocating energy from louder to quieter parts of the signal increases intelligibility in noise — this is the mechanistic basis for modulation-envelope/clear-speech DSP enhancement and is portable across engines because it operates on the output waveform.
claims.quote: DMOD ... enhances the temporal modulations of speech which are a key aspect of speech perception [19],[20],[21] and intelligibility [22], and hence significantly increases word recognition in noise. Rather than using static input-output curves ... DMOD uses a mathematical equation to increase the energy of quieter parts compared to louder parts of speech ... This technique increases the low-frequency temporal modulations of speech and thus significantly improves intelligibility.

claims.claim: Modulation/temporal-envelope enhancement can run in real-time at very low latency (approximately 2 ms processing delay), making it compatible with on-device/streaming pipelines rather than requiring whole-file offline processing.
claims.quote: RMOD is inspired by the DMOD [12], however it performs real-time energy reallocation (approx 2ms delay) and uses novel scaling operations to maintain speech quality.

claims.claim: There is a measurable trade-off between intelligibility gain and speech quality in envelope-enhancement DSP: the more aggressive SSDRC achieved higher intelligibility gains in all tested cases but degraded quality (negative subjective preference), whereas the gentler RMOD preserved quality but only approached SSDRC's intelligibility.
claims.quote: the intelligibility gains of RMOD approach those of SSDRC for both the competing speaker and the canteen noise, although the SSDRC has higher intelligibility gains in all cases ... RMOD and unmodified speech appear to have similar preference scores, whereas SSDRC gives negative quality scores.

claims.claim: Intelligibility gains from envelope/spectral-shaping DSP enhancement are substantially smaller under realistic recorded noise than under synthetic speech-shaped noise, so benefits measured in idealized lab conditions overstate real-world gains.
claims.quote: Results suggest that the intelligibility gains for both algorithms are quite low when tested with real noises. This contrasts with previous results obtained for SSDRC on SSN and demonstrates the importance of testing speech enhancement algorithms in wide range of realistic conditions.

claims.claim: Intelligibility-enhancement DSP should be evaluated with both objective metrics (STOI, extended Speech Intelligibility Index, Glimpse Portion, POLQA) and subjective preference/listening tests, because some metrics capture intelligibility only while others jointly account for quality and distortion.
claims.quote: Several objective measures of intelligibility were used ... the extended Speech Intelligibility Index (extSII,[25]), the Glimpse Portion model (GP,[26]), the Distortion Weighted Glimpse Portion model (DWGP,[4]) and the Short-Time Objective Intelligibility measure (STOI,[27]). While GP and extSII assess intelligibility, DWGP and STOI are correlated with both intelligibility and quality.

content.text:
Extracted the source successfully. Summary of what I found:

The ResearchGate URL returned HTTP 403, but I located the same paper on the authoritative ISCA archive (Interspeech 2017) and extracted the full text from the PDF saved at `/Users/fred/.claude/projects/-Users-fred-Software-Engineering-HSC-Textbook-podcast-content/6b1689f5-87d0-40e5-9143-cb0867480812/tool-results/bcajjneaf.txt`.

Source quality: primary (peer-reviewed Interspeech 2017 conference paper, Samsung Electronics; Koutsogiannaki, Francois, Choo, Oh).

The paper is most relevant to research angle 4 (DSP enhancement for intelligibility) and angle 6 (measurement). Key takeaways for the pipeline: modulation-envelope enhancement (boosting low-frequency temporal modulations by shifting energy from loud to quiet segments) is a real-time, low-latency (~2 ms), waveform-level technique applicable to any TTS engine's output. But it carries two important cautions: (1) a measurable intelligibility-vs-quality trade-off (aggressive SSDRC wins intelligibility but loses quality; gentler RMOD preserves quality but only approaches SSDRC's gains), and (2) gains shrink markedly under realistic recorded noise versus synthetic speech-shaped noise. These nuances are useful for setting realistic expectations on the "DSP post-processing" effort tier and underscore validating with both objective (STOI, extSII, GP, POLQA) and subjective listening tests.
